file_name: Rout et al. - 2022 - Content-based medical image retrieval system for skin melanoma diagnosis based on optimized pair-wis.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an optimized pair-wise comparison (OPWC) approach combined with meta-heuristic optimization algorithms (Particle Swarm Optimization, JAYA, Genetic Algorithm, and Gray Wolf Optimization) and Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) to dynamically weight features for a Content-Based Medical Image Retrieval (CBMIR) system. This computational method was applied to 2300 dermoscopic images to assist in the identification and classification of 23 types of skin melanoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL PAPER' and presents a new Content-Based Medical Image Retrieval (CBMIR) system. It details original experimental work involving the development of an optimized pair-wise comparison (OPWC) approach, the application of meta-heuristic algorithms (PSO, JAYA, GA, GWO), and the evaluation of these methods on a dataset of 2300 dermoscopic images to achieve specific precision and recall results.

title: Content‑Based Medical Image Retrieval System for Skin Melanoma Diagnosis Based on Optimized Pair‑Wise Comparison Approach
brief_summary: This study developed a Content-Based Medical Image Retrieval (CBMIR) system to assist dermatologists in accurately identifying 23 types of skin melanoma. The system utilized 2300 dermoscopic images from the DermNet dataset to train a model that dynamically weights low-level features (color, shape, texture) based on individual image characteristics. The dynamic weighting was achieved through an optimized pair-wise comparison (OPWC) approach supported by the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) and meta-heuristic algorithms including Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), JAYA, Genetic Algorithm (GA), and Gray Wolf Optimization (GWO). Evaluation on testing images demonstrated that the JAYA-AHP approach achieved the highest precision of 99.55% and recall of 24.88% with a window size of 25. The results indicate that the proposed OPWC system serves as an effective expert assistive tool for improving the accuracy of skin melanoma diagnosis.

The dataset used is DermNet, which contains images of 23 classes, with 'Melanoma' being one of the specific classes (Class 12 in Table 1) among other skin conditions.

all_task_types.target: Similar medical images from a database
all_task_types.details: The paper develops a Content-Based Medical Image Retrieval (CBMIR) system. The model retrieves similar images from a database of 2300 dermoscopic images based on dynamically weighted low-level features (color, shape, texture) using an Optimized Pair-Wise Comparison (OPWC) approach. Performance is evaluated using precision and recall metrics for retrieving similar cases to assist in diagnosis, rather than directly predicting a discrete class label or continuous value for the query image itself.

datasets_used.name: DermNet
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic images of skin diseases, downloaded from Kaggle (https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/shubhamgoel27/dermnet). It contains images of 23 different classes of skin conditions/melanoma types.
classes_used: Basal Cell Carcinoma and other Malignant Lesions
Lupus and other Connective Tissue diseases
Psoriasis and other Lichen Planus related diseases
Tinea (Ringworm, Candidiasis and other Fungal Infections)
Vascular Tumors (Molluscum and other Viral Infections)

use.training: 100 random images were taken from each of the 23 classes (total 2300 images) from the training group (15,592 images) for training the skin-melanoma similar image search system.
use.testing: 10 random images were taken from each of the 23 classes (total 230 images) from the testing group (4,002 images) for testing the system. Additionally, 10 random images from each class of non-database images were used for testing retrieval performance.
use.other: The full database (15,592 training + 4,002 testing) served as the source pool from which subsets were randomly selected for the experiment.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To determine the statistical significance of the optimization results and rank the performance of different OPWC approaches (GA-AHP, PSO-AHP, GWO-AHP, JAYA-AHP) based on precision and recall.
To justify the user's judgment in the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) pair-wise comparison matrix and ensure the reliability of the calculated feature weights.
Used as the threshold for the Friedman statistical test to assume reduction performance of specified algorithms.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in the performance (precision and recall) among the different OPWC approaches (GA-AHP, PSO-AHP, GWO-AHP, and JAYA-AHP).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the performance (precision and recall) among the different OPWC approaches.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The Friedman statistical test assumes reduction performance of specified algorithms at a significance level of 0.05. The results indicated statistical significance, allowing the ranking of the optimized AHP algorithms in descending order based on precision and recall values (e.g., JAYA-AHP ranked highest for testing images).

DermNet (Training Images).description: Results for 2300 training images (100 random images from each of 23 classes). Metrics are Average Precision (AP) and Average Recall (AR) calculated over a window size of 25.

statistical_ranking.ranking_order_descending_performance: PSO-AHP (Rank I), GWO-AHP (Rank II), JAYA-AHP (Rank III), GA-AHP (Rank IV)

DermNet (Training Images).notes: Classes 1, 5, 10, 11, and 21 were noted as having images difficult to match with similar images. Shape feature weight dominated other features for GA-AHP, PSO-AHP, and GWO-AHP, while color feature weight showed better variance/standard deviation for JAYA-AHP.

DermNet (Testing/Non-Database Images).description: Results for 230 testing images (10 random images from each of 23 classes) plus 10 random images from each class of non-database images. Metrics are Average Precision (AP) and Average Recall (AR) calculated over a window size of 25.

statistical_ranking.ranking_order_descending_performance: JAYA-AHP (Rank I), PSO-AHP (Rank II), GWO-AHP (Rank III), GA-AHP (Rank IV)

DermNet (Testing/Non-Database Images).notes: JAYA-AHP performed best on testing images. The paper concludes JAYA optimization is the better option for smaller numbers of images. Classes 1, 5, 10, 11, and 21 were noted as difficult to match.

Comparative Analysis (Table 8).description: Comparison of the proposed OPWC approach with other state-of-the-art methods.

Comparative Analysis (Table 8).notes: The bold entries in the original table indicate the proposed approach outperforms previous approaches.

architecture_used.main_model: Content-Based Medical Image Retrieval (CBMIR) system using Optimized Pair-Wise Comparison (OPWC) approach
architecture_used.input_size: Variable resolution (images stored in JPEG format, different resolutions)

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Low-level features: Color (color moments, color histogram, color-correlogram), Shape (moments of invariants, eccentricity, solidity, circularity, Zernike moments), Texture (first-order-feature, Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM), Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT))
architecture_details.weighting_mechanism: Dynamic weights assigned to features based on individual image characteristics using Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) and meta-heuristic optimization algorithms
architecture_details.comparison_method: Pair-wise comparison matrix with consistency check (Consistency Index CI and Consistency Ratio CR <= 0.1)

training_details.dataset: DermNet (subset of 2300 images: 100 random images from each of 23 classes)
training_details.training_method: Offline feature vector creation for database images; online feature extraction and comparison for query images

classification_or_output_head.details: Retrieves similar images from the database based on dynamically weighted feature vectors; no discrete classification label output head

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: GA-AHP (Genetic Algorithm combined with Analytic Hierarchy Process)
PSO-AHP (Particle Swarm Optimization combined with Analytic Hierarchy Process)
GWO-AHP (Gray Wolf Optimization combined with Analytic Hierarchy Process)
JAYA-AHP (JAYA algorithm combined with Analytic Hierarchy Process)
Transfer Learning with pre-trained Xception model [Anand et al.]
preprocessing_techniques: No specific preprocessing techniques (such as resizing, cropping, normalization, or denoising) were reported; images were used in their original JPEG format with varying resolutions and normal quality.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper did not report any data augmentation techniques (such as geometric transforms, color transforms, or synthetic data generation) used during training or dataset construction. The study utilized a fixed subset of 2300 dermoscopic images randomly selected from the DermNet dataset (100 images per class) without applying augmentation methods.

conclusions: The proposed Content-Based Medical Image Retrieval (CBMIR) system using the Optimized Pair-Wise Comparison (OPWC) approach serves as an effective expert assistive tool to help dermatologists and physicians accurately identify different types of skin melanoma.

The OPWC approach dynamically assigns weights to low-level features (color, shape, texture) based on individual image characteristics, which is more effective than applying static weights for handling the variability in medical images.

Among the tested meta-heuristic optimization algorithms combined with Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP), the JAYA-AHP approach achieved the highest performance on testing (non-database) images, with a precision of 99.55% and recall of 24.88%.
The JAYA optimization algorithm is concluded to be the better option for scenarios involving smaller numbers of images, as it outperformed PSO-AHP, GWO-AHP, and GA-AHP on the testing dataset.
On the training database images, the PSO-AHP approach ranked highest in the Friedman average ranking test, followed by GWO-AHP, JAYA-AHP, and GA-AHP.

Certain classes, specifically acne and Rosacea, cellulitis impetigo and other bacterial infections, light diseases and disorders of pigmentation, lupus and other connective tissue diseases, and vascular tumors, were identified as difficult to match with similar images, indicating areas for potential system improvement.

The shape feature weight dominated other features for GA-AHP, PSO-AHP, and GWO-AHP approaches, whereas the color feature weight showed better variance and standard deviation for the JAYA-AHP approach.

The proposed system significantly reduces the semantic gap and retrieval time by allowing physicians to search past similar medical records from a large database, thereby supporting accurate diagnosis and treatment decisions.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, randomly sampled subset of the DermNet dataset (2,300 training and 230 testing images) rather than the full dataset, which may limit the generalizability of the results.

The system is a Content-Based Medical Image Retrieval (CBMIR) tool, not a direct diagnostic classifier; it retrieves similar past cases to assist physicians, which introduces a dependency on the quality and diversity of the reference database.

The proposed approach involves intensive computation, leading to potential delays in online or real-time clinical deployment; the authors acknowledge the need for less computation-intensive algorithms for practical use.

Retrieval performance was notably poor for specific classes (Acne/Rosacea, Cellulitis, Light Diseases, Lupus, and Vascular Tumors), indicating significant bias or difficulty in matching images for these conditions.

The dataset contains images with varying resolutions and normal quality (not extremely high), and no specific preprocessing (e.g., resizing or normalization) was applied, which may affect feature extraction consistency.
No data augmentation techniques were employed during the training or testing phases.
The paper compares its retrieval metrics (Precision/Recall) against other studies that report classification metrics (Accuracy), making direct performance comparisons methodologically inconsistent.

The system was tested on 'non-database' images (10 random images from each class not in the training set) to simulate real-world query scenarios, but the sample size per class (n=10) is very small for robust statistical evaluation.
